1:
The witnesses stand together in grief over the opening indictment, and they divide only over how sharply to read one clause. The Reformed Gill states the charge without softening: Solomon's was a lustful and not a lawful love, fixed on women who were foreign, unlawful, and many. The Puritan Matthew Henry turns it into lament — shall Solomon fall, who was the beauty of Israel and so great a blessing of his generation? He hears the fall echo down the centuries (Neh. 13:26), traces it to the flesh, and warns from the same Scriptures Solomon himself had penned: give not thy strength unto women (Prov. 31:3), for the love of women has cast down many wounded (Prov. 7:26). Henry marks the descent by stages — he doted on strange women, took many, chose them from forbidden nations, and at last clave unto them in love; and he draws the sober rule that lesser sins, made light of, open the door to greater, for David had multiplied wives too, and one bad act of a good man may do more harm than twenty of a wicked one.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the legal ground of the blame. Keil and Delitzsch insist the daughter of Pharaoh is set apart from the censure — the phrase means along with her, so that the Egyptian marriage is distinguished from the foreign wives who turned his heart. What Scripture blames is the breach of the royal law that a king should not multiply wives (Deuteronomy 17:17), and marriage with nations the law had fenced off (Deuteronomy 7:1–3; Exodus 34:16). Ellicott sharpens the point: Moab, Ammon, and Edom were kindred to Israel of Abraham's stock, while the Hittites and Sidonians were of the old Canaanite line to whom the prohibition strictly attached; yet the reason of the law now applied to all, since all had fallen to false gods, an extension the returned exiles openly recognised (Ezra 9:2; Nehemiah 13:23–29).

Here the one real division appears. The Wesleyan Clarke refuses to exempt the Egyptian: strange women means idolaters, and Pharaoh's daughter was one of them; many think she became a proselyte, he grants, but of that there is no evidence. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott, by contrast, note that no Egyptian idolatry is ever charged to Solomon, and so leave her marriage outside the wreck. The debate is narrow and the verdict one: whether one wife be excused or not, the multiplying of forbidden loves was the hinge on which the wisest of kings began to turn from his God.
2:
This verse supplies the law's own reason for the fence Solomon broke, and the witnesses read it as the seed of everything that follows. Both voices attend first to the divine warning quoted here: the Lord had said of these nations, ye shall not go in to them, neither shall they come in unto you, for surely they will turn away your heart after their gods. The Reformed Gill ties the prohibition specifically to the Hittites, one of the seven Canaanite nations against whom the command in the Law was directed; the danger named was not political but spiritual — the certainty that intermarriage would end in apostasy.

That certainty is exactly what the narrative is about to prove. The verse pointedly closes with the words Solomon clave unto these in love, and the witnesses feel the weight of the verb. It is the language of covenant attachment — the very word Scripture uses for a man cleaving to his wife — here bent toward the forbidden. Solomon did not merely tolerate these women; he was bound to them in affection, and the affection carried his heart where the Law had foreseen it would go.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the account forward into the scale of the thing. The seven hundred wives who were princesses, women exalted into the first rank, and the three hundred concubines are round numbers, they observe, gathered across the whole of his reign rather than present at any one moment; and even so they dwarf the sixty queens and eighty concubines glimpsed elsewhere in his court (Song of Solomon 6:8). The commentators do not contend here; they simply let the verse do its own grim work. The command had told him precisely what would happen, and told him why; the closing clause records that he did the forbidden thing anyway, and did it not by cold policy but by love. Everything the chapter goes on to relate — the strange altars, the divine anger, the torn kingdom — is contained in embryo in this quiet notice that Solomon clave, in love, to the very women God had warned would turn his heart away.
3:
The bare arithmetic of the harem — seven hundred wives who were princesses and three hundred concubines — draws the witnesses into a genuine dispute over how the numbers should be taken, even as they agree on the ruin they worked. The Reformed Gill calls the thousand a prodigious number, but suggests that many were kept for state after the manner of eastern monarchs rather than for use. The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the same line further: an Eastern king's harem is an adjunct of his magnificence, and the relation of most of these wives to him little more than nominal. Yet Ellicott will not simply excuse the figures. They are extraordinarily large, he says, excessive beside the far smaller company named elsewhere (Song of Solomon 6:8), and oddly disproportioned between the higher and lower ranks; and though the same numbers stand in the Septuagint and in Josephus, he judges it possible that the text has suffered an ancient corruption.

The Wesleyan Clarke will hear none of these mitigations. He wonders aloud how so many could be gathered of royal blood from the surrounding nations — allowing that daughters of nobles and generals may be included — and defines the concubines as wives of the second rank whose offspring could not inherit, standing to Solomon as Hagar and Keturah stood to Abraham. But he presses the moral question the others soften: was it possible that such a person could have any piety to God, who was absorbed by such a number of women? We scarcely allow the fear of God to a man with a second wife, he says; in what state then must a man be with a thousand? He refuses the plea of custom, insisting that Solomon loved these women, clave to them in love, and gave the fullest proof of his attachment when he tolerated and even built temples for their gods — Ashtoreth, Milcom, Chemosh, Molech — so that he seems to have gone as far in iniquity as it was possible.

The division, then, is one of temper more than of doctrine. Gill and Ellicott read the number as partly a matter of oriental display, and Ellicott even suspects the copyists; Clarke takes it at full weight as the measure of a great and deliberate apostasy. All three, however, arrive at the same closing clause the verse itself supplies — his wives turned away his heart — and none of them doubts that the multitude of these loves was the engine of the fall.
4:
The witnesses fasten on the mournful timing — when Solomon was old — and on the exact measure of his failure, that his heart was not perfect with the Lord his God, as was the heart of David his father. The Reformed Gill reckons him near sixty at the time, since his son Rehoboam was forty-one at his accession, and finds the age an aggravation: the man who by long experience should have grown wiser and less lustful grew instead more foolish. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the phrase precisely: in old age, when the flesh gained the mastery over the spirit, his wives turned his heart, so that it was no longer shalem, wholly given to the Lord (1 Kings 8:61). The comparison with David is careful, not naive — David had sinned grievously, they note, yet never fell into idolatry, and that is the line Solomon crossed.

Ellicott, also of the grammatical-historical school, opens the chapter's most searching question about the very nature of the apostasy. It is not likely, he argues, that Solomon simply forsook the worship of God, for the temple service went on (1 Kings 9:25); rather his idolatry was an eclectic tolerance, an inclination to adopt the various forms of faith as so many phases of reverence toward one Supreme Power. Such a spirit — holding no creed, but contemplating and condescending to all — was the natural fruit of an overweening confidence in his own wisdom, of despotic power, of world-wide fame, and of an over-luxurious magnificence. Ellicott even hears in it a kinship with the weary verdict that all is vanity, since there is something akin between believing that all worships are true and believing that all are false; and it may have seemed shrewd policy to conciliate the subject races, much as imperial Rome later honoured every religion once faith in its own had died. Yet he is emphatic that such a temper is, if anything, more monstrous than open devotion to false gods, and utterly incompatible with the faith of Israel, as the prophet would thunder (Ezekiel 14:3–4).

The voices do not so much disagree as work at different depths. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch name the plain fact — a divided heart, no longer perfect, unlike David's; Ellicott probes the inner disease that made a wise man capable of it. Together they hold that the tragedy of the verse is not a sudden lapse but a slow cooling, the heart that once prayed at the temple's dedication now grown only half the Lord's.
5:
Named at last, the idols draw the witnesses into careful description and into one sharp exegetical caution. The Reformed Gill sees Solomon enticed by the Zidonian women after Ashtoreth, the Astarte whom the Greeks called Venus and reckoned a goddess of the moon. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch enlarge the identification — Astarte was the chief female deity of all the Canaanite tribes, a moon-goddess later carried to Carthage, worshipped as Aphrodite, Urania, and Juno — and they distinguish the Ammonite Milcom of this verse from the Molech to whom children were burned, a distinction the spelling itself preserves.

Here Keil and Delitzsch raise the point on which the witnesses must be weighed against the plain sound of the text. Comparing the fuller notice that Solomon built altars for his wives' gods (v.7), they observe that Scripture nowhere states he personally offered sacrifice upon them; it was the wives who burnt incense. Yet even the building of the altars, they insist, was a participation in idolatry irreconcilable with true fidelity to the Lord — so that the softer reading yields no softer verdict.

Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, gives the fullest anatomy of the cults, and it is an anatomy of horror. Ashtoreth, the receptive power of nature answering to Baal's active one, the queen of heaven (Jeremiah 44:17), was served with chartered license and impurity. Milcom, or Molech, whose name means only king, was the fire-god of the ghastly rite in which children passed through the fire, forbidden in the Law (Leviticus 18:21; compare Psalms 106:37–38) and practised even by Carthage in national calamity. Chemosh, the god of the Moabites and their people's very name (Numbers 21:29; Jeremiah 48:7), was a god of battles to whom, the Moabite record shows, the slain were offered. Ellicott notes how readily such polytheism welcomed strange gods and slid toward a henotheism in which each deity was for the moment worshipped as if he stood alone. The witnesses do not divide on the substance: whether Solomon lit the altars himself or only raised them, he set the impurity of Ashtoreth and the blood of Molech beside the pure and jealous worship of the God of Israel, and could not serve both.
6:
This verse pronounces the deuteronomic verdict on Solomon in its flattest form — Solomon did evil in the sight of the Lord, and went not fully after the Lord, as did David his father — and the two witnesses present treat it as the summary sentence toward which everything since the first verse has been moving. The Reformed Gill answers the charge with a single, weighty equation: idolatry is evil, and nothing is more provoking to God than that. He does not soften the language into mere weakness or lapse; the verse uses the covenant word for wickedness, and Gill lets it stand, because to divide the heart between the Lord and Ashtoreth is not a lesser thing than open rebellion but the very thing the Law most abhorred.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the heading of the paragraph that follows, and gather into it the concrete acts by which the evil was done. Solomon went not fully after the Lord in that he built a high place for Chemosh on the hill before Jerusalem and for Molech, and did the like for all his foreign wives. These three altars, they note — the very ones later named when Josiah tore them down (2 Kings 23:13) — sufficed for all the deities of the harem, since the Hittites and Edomites seem to have had no gods peculiar to themselves, the one people worshipping Astarte with the Sidonians and the other Milcom with the Ammonites.

The force of the verse, for both witnesses, lies in the pointed comparison it draws: as did David his father. The measure of Solomon's failure is not an abstract standard but a living one — the father whose heart, for all his sins, had never followed other gods. Solomon's tragedy is stated not as gross wickedness in the ordinary sense but as a falling short of a known and nearer pattern; he did not renounce the Lord outright so much as he ceased to follow him fully. That half-heartedness is precisely what the sacred writer names as evil, and it is what the building of the high places, described in the verses following, made visible for all Jerusalem to see.
7:
The witnesses read this verse for its geography and its affront, and they are of one mind on both. The place of the new shrines is fixed with unusual care: Solomon built a high place for Chemosh in the hill that is before Jerusalem. The Reformed Gill, following Jarchi, names it the Mount of Olives, afterward called for this very reason the mount of corruption; and he notes that the shrine was raised for the Moabite god at the instigation of Solomon's Moabite women, with a like altar for Molech, the abomination of the children of Ammon. The Wesleyan Clarke confirms the site in a word — the hill before Jerusalem was the Mount of Olives.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws out what the location signified. The high places stood on the Mount of Olives, part of which still traditionally bears the name of the Mount of Offence, facing and rivalling the Temple that crowned Mount Moriah across the valley. The insult was deliberate in its very setting: the shrines of Chemosh and Molech looked the house of the Lord in the face. Ellicott adds the topography of the fouler rite, noting that Tophet, the actual place of sacrifice to Molech, lay in the valley of the son of Hinnom (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31), at the foot of the same mountain, so that the whole eastern approach to the city became a landscape of idolatry.

The commentators do not contend; they let the geography carry the weight of the indictment. That the wisest of kings, who had himself built and dedicated the Temple with that great prayer, should now set the altars of the surrounding nations on the ridge directly opposite it, is a picture the witnesses find more eloquent than any denunciation. The shrines were not hidden in the harem or tucked into a private garden; they stood upon the hill before Jerusalem, an open counter-temple to the God of Israel, and they endured there, a standing reproach, until Josiah at last defiled and demolished them (2 Kings 23:13) three centuries on.
8:
The verse widens the ruin to its full extent — likewise did he for all his strange wives, which burnt incense and sacrificed unto their gods — and the witnesses read in that little word all the terrible logic of compromise. The Reformed Gill puts his finger on the mechanism: having built or suffered a high place for one wife's idol, Solomon could not refuse the same to another without greatly disobliging them; each concession made the next unavoidable, until every foreign wife had her shrine. What began as complaisance to one became a policy toward all, and the king who could deny his women nothing found himself the patron of every abomination they had brought from home.

The verse is careful to distinguish the acts. It is the wives who burnt incense and sacrificed unto their gods; Solomon's part was to provide the altars and permit the worship. Yet the witnesses do not let that distinction excuse him, for it was his hand that raised the shrines and his authority that maintained the priests, so that the whole apparatus of idolatry in Jerusalem stood by his leave. The one who had built the house of the Lord now built houses for Ashtoreth and Chemosh and Milcom, and the smoke of strange incense rose across the valley from the house of God.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott lifts the eye from the altars to the astonishing privilege Solomon was despising. The Lord, he reminds us, had appeared unto him twice (1 Kings 3:5; 1 Kings 9:2) — direct visions, set over against the usual indirect revelation through the prophets, and carrying with them a peculiar privilege and a peculiar responsibility. This is the note on which the narrative turns from the record of the sin to the record of its judgment. The reader is meant to hold together the completeness of the apostasy — altars for all his wives — and the singular grace against which it was committed, a grace of face-to-face appearing granted to no one else of his generation. It is that combination, the many idols and the twice-given vision, that makes the divine anger of the next verse not merely just but grievous.
9:
With the divine anger the witnesses turn from the sin to its aggravations, and they are unanimous that what makes the offence so grievous is the privilege against which it was committed. The verse itself supplies the reason: the Lord was angry because his heart was turned from the Lord God of Israel, which had appeared unto him twice. The Reformed Gill reads the turning as a casting off of the fear of God, for a man could not give himself to idolatry in any shape while that fear held; and nothing, he observes, is more provoking to God than this. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay the whole emphasis where the text does — on the twice-repeated appearing (1 Kings 3:5; 1 Kings 9:2). God had warned him against idolatry not through a prophet only but in person, and it is that face-to-face grace, spurned, which draws down the anger of Jehovah.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the aggravations into two. First, the most base ingratitude: God keeps account of the gracious visits he makes us, and Solomon turned from the Lord who had appeared to him twice, once before the temple was built and once after its dedication — appearings that should forever have prevented his worshipping any other god. Second, the most wilful disobedience, for this was the very thing concerning which God had expressly commanded him; and Henry adds the shrewd word that those who have dominion over men are apt to forget God's dominion over them, demanding obedience from their inferiors while denying it to the Supreme.

The Wesleyan Clarke drives the same nail from the side of divine justice. Had Solomon's delinquency not been strongly marked by God's disapprobation, it would have had a fatal effect on the morals of mankind, for vice is vice, no matter who commits it. Clarke concedes that the act itself carries the same moral turpitude in king or peasant, but insists that circumstances may aggravate the guilt and justly increase the punishment: Solomon was wise and knew better, God had appeared to him and fulfilled every promise, and so he deserved more than the Sidonians who worshipped Ashtoreth in ignorance, having never known the true God whom Solomon knew and forsook. The voices do not divide. From Reformed, Puritan, and Wesleyan alike comes one confession — that the sin is measured by the light against which it is done, and that the twice-given vision made Solomon's idolatry not less but immeasurably more inexcusable.
10:
This verse presses the aggravation of the previous one to its sharpest edge, and the two witnesses read it as the record of a sin committed against an explicit, personal prohibition. The text notes that God had commanded him concerning this very thing, that he should not go after other gods; but he kept not that which the Lord commanded. The Reformed Gill takes it as yet another aggravation of his sin — that it was against an express command of God, particularly given to Solomon himself, with a warning to observe it and a threat of evil should he break it. The sin was not one of ignorance or oversight; the terms had been laid down to his face and their penalty spelled out, so that in going after Ashtoreth and Milcom he trampled a word he had personally received.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the second half of the accusation that grounds the sentence, and they let it run straight on into the divine word itself. Solomon kept not that which the Lord commanded, and therefore — forasmuch as this is done of thee, and thou hast not kept my covenant and my statutes which I have commanded thee — the kingdom will be torn from him and given to his servant. The commentators feel the covenantal weight of the language: it is not a private lapse but a breach of covenant, a failure to keep what had been explicitly charged, and the judgment answers exactly to the terms transgressed.

Both witnesses, then, hear in the verse the closing of the case for the prosecution. Everything that might have excused a lesser man is stripped away: the command was clear, it was addressed to him, it concerned this precise danger, and it came reinforced by the twice-repeated appearing. Solomon knew the law, held it from God's own mouth, and broke it deliberately. The verse thus prepares the reader to receive the sentence of the verses following not as arbitrary severity but as the just and measured response of a covenant God to a covenant broken by the one man who, above all others, had least excuse to break it.
11:
The sentence now falls, and the witnesses divide gently over whether even this word of judgment still held out a door of mercy. The verse records the manner of its coming: Wherefore the Lord said unto Solomon. The Reformed Gill marks the change from the earlier revelations — this was not in a vision, as before, but by a prophet; and, following the Jewish tradition, he names the messenger as Ahijah the Shilonite, the prophet who reappears later in the chapter to tear his garment before Jeroboam. The God who had twice appeared in person now speaks through a servant, and the shift itself is eloquent of a relationship strained by sin.

The substance of the message is the tearing away of the kingdom and its gift to Solomon's servant — the very Jeroboam whom the chapter will soon introduce. The justice of it the witnesses feel keenly: having given God's glory to the creature, Solomon must see his crown given to his subject; the kingdom he had thought to entail upon his heirs forever will be rent from his house.

Yet the Wesleyan Clarke hears in the opening words something more than sentence. Forasmuch as this is done of thee — was not this, he asks, another warning from the Lord? And might not Solomon have yet recovered himself? Was there not mercy in this message, which he might have sought and found? Clarke reads even the announcement of judgment as an implicit summons to repentance, an open hand within the closing fist, consistent with his conviction that grace is never merely fatalistic in its dealings. The question is left hanging, and deliberately so, for the Scripture does not record Solomon's answer. Whether the threatened king humbled himself under the word or hardened against it, the verse itself does not say; it stands as both sentence and, on Clarke's reading, invitation — the last recorded direct dealing of God with the man he had loved, spoken now at arm's length through a prophet, and awaiting a response the sacred writer withholds from us. Gill's identification of the messenger with Ahijah binds this word to the sign that follows, for the same prophet who here announces the tearing of the kingdom will shortly enact it in the field, rending his garment into twelve pieces before the very servant to whom the ten tribes are promised.
12:
This verse and the next are the mitigation of the sentence, and the witnesses read them as a study in mercy deferred and mercy grounded not in merit but in promise. The word is precise: Notwithstanding in thy days I will not do it, for David thy father's sake; but I will rend it out of the hand of thy son. The Reformed Gill fixes at once on the ground of the reprieve. It was for David thy father's sake — yet, he is careful to say, not for the merits of David, but the promises made to him. The distinction is the heart of the matter: the kingdom is spared to Solomon's lifetime not because David had earned it but because God had pledged it, so that the mercy rests on covenant faithfulness and not on any human deserving (2 Samuel 7:14).

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the shape of the reprieve. The kingdom is rent from Solomon's house, but not immediately: Solomon shall not live to see it done, for it shall be rent out of the hand of his son — and Henry notes the bitter irony that the son who loses it was born to Solomon by one of the very strange wives who ruined him, an Ammonitess (1 Kings 14:31) who likely helped promote the idolatry. He turns it to a settled principle of providence: if judgments be coming, it is a favour to us if they come not in our days, as another king was told when the evil was deferred beyond his lifetime (2 Kings 20:19).

Both witnesses hear in the deferral a mercy that is real but sober. Solomon keeps his throne and dies a king; the stroke falls a generation later, on Rehoboam, in whose reign the ten tribes will pull away. The witnesses do not present this as an escape but as the patience of God, who does not quickly nor wholly take what a man has forfeited. The mercy is genuine — a whole reign of peace is granted still — yet it is a mercy that leaves the sentence standing, only postponed; and it teaches, in Gill's phrase and Henry's, that all the favour God shows to sinful men flows from his promise and his covenant, and never from the worth of those who receive it.
13:
The mitigation completes itself here in the reservation of one tribe, and the witnesses read the clause as the thin bright thread of the Messianic promise running through the dark cloth of judgment. The word limits the loss — Howbeit I will not rend away all the kingdom, but will give one tribe to thy son, for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake which I have chosen. The Reformed Gill simply marks that God will not take the whole kingdom of Israel; a remnant of rule is left to David's line.

The Puritan Matthew Henry fills out the reservation. The one tribe — Judah, the strongest and most numerous, with little Benjamin reckoned into it — remains sure to the house of David, and it is kept for Jerusalem's sake, which David built, and for the sake of the temple there, which Solomon built. Henry finds in the double timing of the whole sentence a divine proportion: Solomon did not quickly nor wholly turn away from God, and therefore God did not quickly nor wholly take the kingdom from him. The judgment is measured to the sin, mercy and severity meeting in exact balance.

The Wesleyan Clarke lifts the clause to its highest sense, and here the witnesses reach their deepest agreement. The one tribe is preserved, he says, because the line of the Messiah must be preserved — the prevailing Lion must come out of the tribe of Judah, and not the tribe only but the regal line and the regal right must be kept, and all of it for the true David's sake. As David was a type of the Messiah, so Jerusalem was a type of the true Church; therefore the old Jerusalem must remain in Judah's hands until the true David should come to establish the New Jerusalem in the same land and city. Clarke marvels at the providence required to accomplish it — to hold Judah and the royal succession intact through so general a defection. The witnesses do not contend. Reformed brevity, Puritan proportion, and Wesleyan typology converge on one confession: that the tribe was spared not for its own sake but for a coming Son of David, and that the mercy shown to Solomon's ruined house was, at bottom, mercy laid up for the world.
14:
With the sentence delivered, the chapter turns to its execution in the raising up of adversaries, and the witnesses read Hadad's appearance through the lens of divine chastening. The verse is explicit that the enmity is God's doing: the Lord stirred up an adversary unto Solomon, Hadad the Edomite. The Reformed Gill notes that though God did not yet take the kingdom, he chastised him with the rod of men, suffering one after another to rise and disturb his peace in old age. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott establish the man: Hadad was of royal Edomite birth, the name a common one in that house and, Ellicott adds, a Syriac title of the sun assumed by kings much as Pharaoh was in Egypt (Genesis 36:35). Hadad, Ellicott judges, was the last scion of the royal line, escaping alone as a child from the slaughter of his kindred.

The Wesleyan Clarke sharpens the word adversary to its Hebrew root — a satan. When Solomon was building the temple he could report that there was no satan, no adversary, and all his kingdom lay in peace, every man under his vine and fig tree (1 Kings 5:4; 1 Kings 4:25); but now that he had turned from God, three satans rise against him at once — Hadad, Rezon, and Jeroboam.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the whole matter into its lesson. While Solomon kept close to God there was no adversary nor evil occurrent; these two enemies were in themselves inconsiderable and could have done nothing worth notice had Solomon not first made God his enemy. What hurt could Hadad or Rezon do to so great a king, unless by sin he had made himself mean and weak? Henry presses it home: if God be on our side, we need not fear the greatest adversary; but if he be against us, he can make us fear the least, and the very grasshopper shall be a burden. And he bids us look through the instruments of trouble to their Author, hearing the Lord's controversy in them, as David heard it when Shimei cursed. The voices unite in reading Hadad not as a political accident but as a rod in the hand of a chastening God, and in the sobering truth that sin is what makes a small enemy formidable.
15:
The witnesses turn back a generation to explain Hadad's grudge, and they read the verse as the grim record of Edom's conquest under David. The circumstance is given in a cluster of clauses — when David was in Edom, and Joab the captain of the host was gone up to bury the slain, after he had smitten every male in Edom. The Reformed Gill sets the scene simply: David fighting and subduing the Edomites and putting garrisons in the land. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to a nicety — the slain Joab went up to bury were probably not the Israelites fallen in the battle of the Salt Valley but the Edomites cut down when they had first invaded, so that the burial follows a war of reprisal (compare 2 Samuel 8:13–14).

The Wesleyan Clarke states the severity baldly: Joab in the course of six months exterminated all the males of Edom except Hadad and his servants, who escaped to Egypt; and he notes that the Targum reads not bury the slain but take the spoils of the slain. The grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies the fullest background, gathering the parallel notices where the same war is recorded with differences of detail (2 Samuel 8:12–14; 1 Chronicles 18:11–13; Psalms 60, in its title). The victory is ascribed in one record to Joab and in another to Abishai, and here David himself is described as taking part, perhaps completing a conquest Joab had begun. Ellicott reads the Septuagint's variant, David destroyed Edom, and characterizes the campaign as one of ruthless extermination of every male save those who fled the country or hid in its rocky fastnesses, carried on by systematic ravage under Joab's command; how it was provoked, he confesses, we do not know.

The witnesses do not dispute; they assemble the history that the verse presupposes. And the effect of their labour is to set Solomon's present trouble in a long moral perspective. The rod that now scourges the son was forged in the father's severity; the child who escaped Joab's sword grew into the adversary of Solomon's old age. Henry's principle from the preceding verse hangs over the scene — that those who are free in giving provocation may find it remembered and returned with interest to their children after them — and the witnesses, simply by narrating the slaughter, let the reader feel how the sins and severities of one age become the thorns of the next.
16:
The verse dwells on the thoroughness of Joab's campaign, and the two witnesses read it as the terrible backdrop against which Hadad's escape becomes visible. The note is exact: for six months did Joab remain there with all Israel, until he had cut off every male in Edom. The Reformed Gill marks only that Joab remained with the whole army — the entire host committed to the work for half a year. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the story straight on into the flight it made necessary: that Hadad fled, he and certain Edomites of his father's servants with him, to go into Egypt, Hadad being yet a little child; and that they journeyed from Midian to Paran, took men from Paran as guides through the desert, and came at last to Pharaoh, who gave them a house, appointed them victuals, and assigned them land.

The force of the verse lies in its arithmetic of destruction. Six months, the whole army, every male cut off — this is not a battle but a systematic extinction, a war meant to leave Edom without a future. The witnesses feel the weight of it precisely because of what escapes it. Into that half-year of slaughter the narrative drops a single surviving child of the royal house, preserved by a handful of his father's servants and carried out of the land by night. The very completeness of Joab's work throws the escape into relief: where every male was to die, one lived, and that one would return.

The witnesses do not moralize the campaign here so much as let its scale register. Keil and Delitzsch note the hospitable reception in Egypt, where Pharaoh not only sheltered the fugitives but endowed them, giving house and food and land — a kindness that would ripen, in time, into a threat against Israel. The reader is meant to hold two things together: the ruthless efficiency of the conquest that seemed to end Edom's line, and the small, providential remainder that God preserved through it. What Joab could not finish, the six months and the whole army notwithstanding, was the purpose of God, who kept alive in a fugitive child the instrument by which he would one day chasten the house that had ordered the slaughter.
17:
The single surviving heir now takes the stage, and the witnesses read the verse for the quiet marvel of a royal child preserved through a national slaughter. The words are spare — that Hadad fled, he and certain Edomites of his father's servants with him, to go into Egypt; Hadad being yet a little child. The Reformed Gill fixes the timing: Hadad fled while Joab was burying the slain, slipping away in the very hour of the burial that closed the campaign, when the attention of the conquerors was turned from pursuit to the disposal of the dead.

The Wesleyan Clarke attends to the child's age with a philologist's care. The Hebrew naar katan, he says, means a little boy — not an infant carried helpless in arms, but one old enough to be apprehensive of his danger and to make his escape with his father's servants. It is a small point that matters, for it makes Hadad an agent in his own preservation rather than merely a bundle borne away; the boy understood that his life was sought and had the wit to flee.

The witnesses do not contend, and the verse gives them little to divide over; but its very brevity invites reflection on what it quietly reports. A whole royal house has been cut down, every male put to the sword, and out of that extinction the narrative rescues one child of the blood, attended by a faithful remnant of servants, and sets his face toward Egypt. The reader who recalls how Israel's own deliverer was once a fugitive child, and how Israel's kings were more than once preserved as infants through slaughter, will feel the pattern without needing it spelled out. Providence keeps its instruments through improbable escapes. The boy who fled while Joab buried the dead was being preserved not for Edom's sake but for a purpose hidden in the future — to be, in his manhood, one of the rods by which God would visit the sins of Solomon; and the servants who carried him to safety were, unknowing, serving that larger design.
18:
The verse traces the fugitives' route out of Edom to their reception in Egypt, and the witnesses read it for both its geography and its politics. The path is marked stage by stage — they arose out of Midian, and came to Paran, and they took men with them out of Paran, and they came to Egypt, unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, which gave him a house, and appointed him victuals, and gave him land. The Reformed Gill notes only that Midian lay in their way and that they made some stay there before departing. The Wesleyan Clarke fills in the itinerary: they retired first to Midian, southwest of the Dead Sea, then, not reckoning themselves safe, to Paran in the south of Idumea, where they gathered a company to join them before going straight down to Egypt, and there Hadad became a favourite whom Pharaoh incorporated into his own family.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs the difficulties and draws out the larger design. The starting point out of Midian is a curious expression, since the flight began in Edom itself; he suggests either a branch of the Midianites settled between Edom and the desert, or a city Midian near the Gulf of Elath to which the Septuagint points. Paran he locates in the Sinaitic region (Genesis 21:21; Numbers 10:12; 1 Samuel 25:1), then inhabited by an independent race from which the fugitives enlisted support.

But Ellicott's chief interest is the Pharaoh who received them. The reigning house, he judges, was the Tanite dynasty, and chronology suggests this king was not the same Pharaoh who became Solomon's father-in-law; the intervening marriage alliance and the joint action against Gezer (1 Kings 9:16) mark a variation of policy. Yet the underlying aim was constant — to secure Egypt's northeastern flank by alliances — and jealousy of Israel's growing power under David and Solomon would prompt this favourable welcome of an Edomite claimant, as later it would prompt the welcome of Jeroboam. The lavishness of the reception, Ellicott concludes, and the eventual marriage of Hadad into the royal house, argue how highly Egypt prized influence over the strong country of Edom and the future chance of Hadad's recovery of its throne. The witnesses thus turn a bare travel notice into a glimpse of the international chessboard on which God was quietly positioning a rod for Solomon's back.
19:
The verse records the astonishing favour Hadad won at the Egyptian court, and the witnesses read it as the elevation of a fugitive into the royal house of Israel's ancient oppressor. The words are plain: Hadad found great favour in the sight of Pharaoh, so that he gave him to wife the sister of his own wife, the sister of Tahpenes the queen. The Reformed Gill supplies the natural cause, supposing the favour owed to Hadad's comely person and princely qualities and good behaviour as he grew up — a distressed young prince who commended himself by his bearing.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the account forward through the marriage to its fruit: the sister of Tahpenes bore Hadad a son, Genubath, whom the queen herself weaned in Pharaoh's house, so that the child was reared among the sons of Pharaoh. When at length Hadad heard in Egypt that David and Joab were dead, he asked Pharaoh's leave to depart to his own country — a request that sets up the exchange of the following verses. Ellicott, of the same school, adds only that the queen Tahpenes is a name otherwise unknown, unattested in history or in the Egyptian monuments.

The witnesses do not dispute; the verse offers little to divide over. But its quiet ironies are not lost on them. The Edomite whose whole family Joab had slaughtered is now married into the family of Pharaoh, sheltered and honoured in the very land from which Israel had once been redeemed, and his son is nursed among the royal children of Egypt. The pattern recalls how Egypt had before been both refuge and rival — a house of bondage that could also serve as sanctuary. Here it becomes the incubator of a future adversary: the great favour Hadad found is not merely a personal fortune but a providential arrangement, binding the exiled prince to a power hostile to Israel and preparing him, in comfort and safety, for the day when he would turn homeward to trouble Solomon. The favour of Pharaoh, freely given, was serving a purpose Pharaoh never guessed.
20:
The verse lingers on the son born to Hadad in Egypt, and the witnesses read the small domestic detail for the completeness of the exile's assimilation into Pharaoh's house. The record is brief — the sister of Tahpenes bore him Genubath his son, whom Tahpenes weaned in Pharaoh's house; and Genubath was in Pharaoh's household among the sons of Pharaoh. The Reformed Gill fastens on the name, observing that Genubath signifies stealth, and suggesting the name may have been given in memory of Hadad himself, who had been carried away by stealth from his own land. The child's very name would thus keep alive the story of the father's escape, a whispered claim upon the throne from which he had been stolen.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, noting that Genubath is otherwise unknown, draws out the significance of the weaning. That Tahpenes weaned the boy in the house of Pharaoh — no doubt, he says, with the customary festival that attended such an occasion (compare Genesis 21:8) — indicated the child's formal admittance into the royal family of Egypt. The detail is not idle; it marks Genubath as reared not merely under Pharaoh's roof but as one of his own, with all the standing that implied.

The witnesses do not contend, and the verse asks for none. Yet its accumulation of small honours carries a quiet weight. Hadad, the fugitive of a slaughtered house, is now so wholly woven into Egypt that his son is nursed and feasted among the princes of the land, indistinguishable in privilege from the sons of Pharaoh. The completeness of the welcome measures the completeness of the exile: nothing was lacking to Hadad in Egypt, no honour withheld, no comfort denied. And it is precisely this fullness that makes the next verses so telling, for a man so richly settled will nonetheless ask to leave. The child at ease among Pharaoh's sons is the sign of a home Hadad could have kept — and the measure of the older, deeper pull of his native soil, and of the hidden purpose drawing him back to Edom.
21:
The turning point of Hadad's exile comes with news from home, and the witnesses read the verse for the patience of a claimant who waited for the right hour. The report reaches him plainly: when Hadad heard in Egypt that David slept with his fathers, and that Joab the captain of the host was dead. The Reformed Gill remarks how such tidings travelled — the death of princes is soon known in other countries, and especially the death of a king of David's fame, so that the news of his burial would not be long in reaching Egypt.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose account gathers these verses together, mark what the news prompted. On learning that both David and Joab were gone, Hadad said to Pharaoh, Let me depart, that I may go to mine own country; and Pharaoh answered with the words of a man loath to lose him — What is there lacking to thee with me? — a reply, they note, that is pure expression of love and attachment, carrying within it the unspoken request that Hadad would remain. But Hadad's answer was fixed: No; but let me go.

The witnesses find the timing deeply significant. Hadad did not stir while David lived, nor while Joab, the very hand that had butchered Edom, still commanded Israel's armies; he waited until both were laid in the grave. The two men bound up with his people's slaughter had to be gone before he would move. Gill's observation about the swift travel of royal news joins to Keil and Delitzsch's picture of the reluctant Pharaoh to give the scene its shape: the moment David slept with his fathers, the long-nursed purpose stirred. What had been a comfortable exile now felt like a delay to be ended, and no kindness of Egypt could hold the man once the road home lay open. The reader is meant to see the hand of God in the season of it — the rod kept sheathed through David's reign and drawn only when the sentence upon Solomon had begun to fall, so that the adversary's homeward turn coincides exactly with the season of Solomon's decline.
22:
The exchange between Pharaoh and Hadad is completed here, and the witnesses read the homesick refusal for both its historical reticence and its spiritual lesson. Pharaoh presses again — But what hast thou lacked with me, that behold thou seekest to go to thine own country? — and Hadad answers, Nothing; howbeit let me go in any wise. The Reformed Gill glosses Pharaoh's question as covering everything a prince might want: nothing lacking of equipage suitable to his birth, of provision for his household, of honour and respect, of any favour. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read Pharaoh's words as pure love and attachment, and note that though the text does not expressly say Pharaoh let him go, it must be supplied, as such an outcome is elsewhere left to be inferred (Numbers 10:32). The Wesleyan Clarke adds a shrewd political observation: Hadad did not avow his real intention, for there was then peace between Israel and Egypt, Solomon having married Pharaoh's daughter — so the returning exile kept his purpose to himself.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, who reads the whole episode together, lifts the refusal into a meditation on the soul's true home. Hadad wanted for nothing, yet longed only for his own country, his native air, his native soil; and Henry borrows the pious reflection of Peter Martyr: Heaven is our home, and we ought to keep up a holy affection to that, and desire toward it, even when the world, the place of our banishment, smiles most upon us. When the world asks what we have lacked, that we are so willing to be gone, Henry would have us answer, Nothing that the world can do for us; but still let us go thither, where our hope, and honour, and treasure are.

The witnesses do not divide over the plain sense. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke attend to the surface — the generosity of Pharaoh, the reticence of Hadad, the peace that made his departure delicate; Henry turns the surface into a mirror. The adversary's restlessness in the midst of Egyptian plenty becomes a picture of the believer's proper unease amid earthly comfort, a homeward pull that no abundance can satisfy. That the lesson is drawn from an enemy of Israel only sharpens it: even Hadad knew that a man's own land outweighed a stranger's bounty, and the saints, Henry implies, should know it better of their country above.
23:
The second adversary now enters, and the witnesses read his rise as the fulfilment of an old prophetic threat against David's house. The verse is deliberate about the source of the trouble: God stirred him up another adversary, Rezon the son of Eliadah, which fled from his lord Hadadezer king of Zobah. The Reformed Gill marks the symmetry — one enemy from the south in Hadad, and now another from the north in Rezon — so that Solomon is hemmed in on both frontiers by God's appointment.

The Wesleyan Clarke reads the whole train of adversaries as the express fulfilment of Nathan's word to David concerning the son who should build the temple: if he commit iniquity, I will chasten him with the rod of men, and with the stripes of the children of men (2 Samuel 7:14). Rezon, like Hadad, is one of the men of that rod. The grammatical-historical Ellicott attends to the name and its bearing, noting that Rezon appears to signify prince, a fitting title for the founder of a new power, and that he may be identical with the Hezion later named as king of Damascus (compare 1 Kings 15:18). He records too the tradition preserved by Josephus from Nicolaus of Damascus, that for ten generations from David's day the kings of Syria bore the name Hadad — which he takes to mean only that Hadad was the official title of the Damascus monarchy.

The witnesses do not contend; each adds a facet. Gill sets Rezon geographically opposite Hadad; Clarke sets him theologically under the same prophecy; Ellicott sets him within the dynastic history of Damascus. What emerges from their agreement is a single reading of the verse: that the appearance of Rezon is neither accident nor mere ambition working itself out, but the measured chastening of God, promised long before to David and now unfolding upon his son. The kingdom that God has decreed to tear away is already, in Solomon's own lifetime, being harassed at its edges; and the reader is taught to see in the fugitive captain who fled from Zobah not simply a bold opportunist but a sign that the word spoken over David's throne was being kept to the letter.
24:
The verse recounts how Rezon rose from a fugitive captain to a king in Damascus, and the witnesses read it as the birth of the northern power that would trouble Israel for generations. The stages are compressed — he gathered men unto him, and became captain over a band, when David slew them of Zobah; and they went to Damascus, and dwelt therein, and reigned in Damascus. The Reformed Gill supposes the band was gathered from the scattered forces of Rezon's former master. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reconstruct the sequence: Rezon fled from Hadadezer, probably before that king's complete overthrow, collected a company from the fugitives when David smote the troops of Zobah (2 Samuel 8:3–4), and with it afterward marched on Damascus, seized the city, and made himself king — an event they place toward the close of David's reign or after his death, at the very beginning of Solomon's.

The Wesleyan Clarke traces the same arc with a further turn: Rezon, one of Hadadezer's defeated captains, escaped, lived for a time by plunder, and seized Damascus; but when David subdued Syria he drove Rezon out, only for Rezon to recover the city after Solomon's defection, finding that God had departed from Israel, and to join with Hadad in harassing the kingdom — though some, Clarke notes, have thought Hadad and Rezon one and the same man. The grammatical-historical Ellicott fills in the standing of Zobah, a powerful state at war with Hamath and holding supremacy over the Syrians of Damascus, whose vast treasures David captured (2 Samuel 8:1–13); David set governors in Damascus and reduced it to tribute, so that Rezon's independent power there must date only from the later days of Solomon.

The witnesses agree in substance and differ only in the precise timing of Rezon's establishment. What their combined account makes plain is the long shadow the verse casts. The captain who gathered a masterless band in the wake of David's victories becomes the founder of the Syrian kingdom of Damascus, a realm that will war against Israel down the length of the books of Kings. Solomon's decline opens the door; the treasure and territory his father had won are not lost, but their security is broken, and the enemy David had crushed rises again to reign where David had once placed his governors.
25:
The summary of Rezon's enmity closes the account of the two foreign adversaries, and the witnesses read it for the persistence of his hatred and the textual puzzle in its final clause. The verse states the span and the temper of it — he was an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon, beside the mischief that Hadad did; and he abhorred Israel, and reigned over Syria. The Reformed Gill qualifies the phrase all the days of Solomon to mean not the whole of his life but all his days from his first going into idolatry to the end — the enmity effective only once Solomon's defence had departed from him.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that the clause beside the mischief that Hadad did is curiously abrupt in its sudden recurrence to Hadad, and that the text here is doubtful; on the general reading of the Septuagint, he observes, Edom would be substituted for Syria, a change involving only a slight difference in the Hebrew, though the reading of the received text may stand if the explanation of Josephus is correct. He leaves the crux open, honest about the uncertainty.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, gathering both adversaries into one reflection, dwells on the word abhorred. When David had conquered the Syrians, Rezon headed the remnant, lived by spoil and rapine until Solomon grew careless, then seized Damascus and reigned, creating troubles for Israel — probably in concert with Hadad — through all Solomon's later days. And Henry marks the strange note that he abhorred Israel: other princes loved and admired Israel and Solomon and courted their friendship, yet here was one who abhorred them. From this he draws a sober general truth — that the greatest and best of princes and peoples, however widely respected, will yet perhaps be hated and abhorred by some. The witnesses do not divide over the sense. Gill fixes the enmity to the years of apostasy, Ellicott flags the doubtful text, and Henry universalizes the abhorrence; together they leave the reader with a picture of a reign once so admired that queens came from afar to hear its wisdom, now flanked on north and south by settled and implacable hatred — the outward and visible sign of an inward and spiritual defection.
26:
The third and gravest adversary now appears, and the witnesses greet him with a mixture of dread and shrewd observation. The verse names him with ominous fullness — Jeroboam the son of Nebat, an Ephrathite of Zereda, Solomon's servant, whose mother's name was Zeruah, a widow woman, even he lifted up his hand against the king. The Reformed Gill first clears away a rabbinic confusion, noting that this Jeroboam is not, as some Jewish writers held, Sheba the son of Bichri, who was of Benjamin and lost his head to Joab, whereas Jeroboam was of Ephraim.

The Puritan Matthew Henry hears in the name the whole future tragedy: this is Jeroboam the son of Nebat, that made Israel to sin, the man God had told Solomon would receive the greater part of the kingdom. Henry traces his rise as a lesson in industry rewarded. Solomon, having work to do, employed proper persons, and observing Jeroboam to be an industrious young man who minded his business and did it with all his might, advanced him by degrees until he made him overseer of all the charge of the house of Joseph, the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh. Industry is the way to preferment, Henry concludes; the diligent man shall stand before kings and not remain among the mean (Proverbs 22:29). He marks a telling difference among the kings in whom they promoted: Saul took to himself the valiant, Solomon preferred the industrious, but David's eyes were upon the faithful of the land, that they might dwell with him (Psalms 101:6).

The Wesleyan Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott read the same rise but stress the discontent that fed it. Clarke sets Jeroboam over the labourers at Millo and observes that Solomon, at first employing none of Israel in drudgery, grew tyrannical and oppressive as he grew profane, so that at Millo the seeds of disaffection were sown, and the clever, enterprising Jeroboam knew how to avail himself of the general discontent. Ellicott paints the fuller portrait — the great rebel against the house of David, leader of the revolution that divided Israel, introducer of the calf-worship at Dan and Bethel — and notes that the phrase son of a widow woman, added to Solomon's servant, is designed to mark the utterly dependent condition from which Solomon's own favour raised the future rebel. The witnesses thus agree in the irony they find here: the very industry Solomon rewarded, and the very burdens Solomon imposed, together forged the man who would tear away his kingdom.
27:
The verse states the occasion of Jeroboam's revolt, and the witnesses read it as the material grievance behind the prophecy that follows. The cause is tied to Solomon's building works — Solomon built Millo, and repaired the breaches of the city of David his father. The Reformed Gill takes the phrase broadly, finding the occasion of Jeroboam's lifting up his hand both in his advancement to office and in what afterward passed between him and the prophet Ahijah.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the syntax with care, insisting that the whole clause looks forward: the lifting up of the hand announced here is explained by everything from this verse through the prophet's oracle, and it belongs to the time when Jeroboam had become overseer of the heavy works, not to some later moment after the rebellion had begun. The building projects and the labour they demanded are thus set as the immediate setting of the revolt. Ellicott, of the same school, dates the works precisely: Millo was built (1 Kings 9:15) after the Temple and the palace, some twenty years into the reign, when the king's delight in magnificent building had grown upon him — and with it, Ellicott adds pointedly, the burdens of the people.

The witnesses converge on a single reading of the verse: that the fortification and repair of Jerusalem, however splendid, were carried on the backs of a conscripted people, and that the forced labour bred the resentment out of which rebellion grew. The breaches of the city of David were mended, but breaches of another kind were being opened in the loyalty of the tribes. Jeroboam, set over the taskwork of the house of Joseph, stood exactly at the point where the king's ambition met the people's grievance; and it was there, in the dust and burden of the works at Millo, that his hand was first lifted. The commentators do not moralize at length here; they let the plain juxtaposition speak — the king building ever more grandly, the people groaning under the levy, and among the overseers a capable and ambitious man taking note. The stage is set, and the prophet is about to appear upon it.
28:
The verse describes the qualities that raised Jeroboam and the office to which they raised him, and the witnesses read it for the double edge of Solomon's patronage. The man is introduced as a mighty man of valour, whom Solomon, seeing the young man that he was industrious, made ruler over all the charge of the house of Joseph. The Reformed Gill takes the phrase to mean great strength of body and fortitude of mind.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott refines it: the expression, like the mighty valiant man applied to the young David (1 Samuel 16:18), has nothing to do with war but signifies simply strong and capable. The charge, he explains, was the taskwork assigned to the levy from Ephraim, and its mention proves something ominous — that the levy first raised for the Temple, perhaps meant as exceptional, had become the precedent for burdens laid now upon the Israelites themselves and not, as at first, upon the subject races only (compare 1 Kings 9:21–22).

But Ellicott's chief interest lies in the prophet who is about to meet this rising man, and here he sounds a note of large significance. In the person of Ahijah the Shilonite, he observes, prophecy emerges from the abeyance that had overshadowed it during the greatness of the monarchy. Even in David's old age, Nathan had appeared chiefly as a counsellor and servant of the king; from Solomon's coronation onward we hear of no prophetic action, for Solomon himself received the visions of the Lord, and upon him, as the wise man, rested the special inspiration of God. Now, however, Ahijah stands forth as the first of a new line of prophets who would resume a paramount influence like that of Samuel or Nathan, protecting the spirituality of the land and demanding of king and people alike submission to the authority of the Lord. The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the meeting of two forces: a strong and capable man lifted by royal favour into the very machinery of oppression, and, stepping toward him out of the shadows, a prophet whose reappearance signals that God is about to take the direction of Israel's history back into his own hands.
29:
The scene of the great prophecy is set, and the witnesses read the verse for the providential meeting in an open field. The circumstance is carefully drawn — at that time when Jeroboam went out of Jerusalem, the prophet Ahijah the Shilonite found him in the way; and he had clad himself with a new garment; and they two were alone in the field. The Reformed Gill considers what errand took Jeroboam out of the city, supposing he was either going to enter upon his new office or, having been to Solomon to pay in the revenues and settle his accounts, was returning to the country to discharge the duties of his charge.

The Wesleyan Clarke confesses that on what errand Jeroboam went out we cannot know, and turns his attention to the prophet who intercepted him. Ahijah, he notes, was one of those who wrote the history of Solomon's reign, as appears from the record of the sources (2 Chronicles 9:29); and Clarke supposes it may have been by Ahijah that God spoke twice to Solomon, and that this same prophet delivered the message of the tearing of the kingdom earlier in the chapter.

The witnesses do not contend; the verse gives little occasion. Yet its details are not idle, and the commentators feel their weight. The meeting is in the way, outside the city, where no court could overhear; the two men are alone in the field, a privacy that recalls how Samuel had once drawn Saul apart to deliver the word of the Lord. And Ahijah has clothed himself in a new garment, a fresh cloak whose purpose the next verse will disclose, for it was worn on purpose to be torn. The reader is meant to sense the deliberateness of it all: the prophet has come out to find Jeroboam, has chosen his time and place, and has dressed for the sign he is about to enact. What looks like a chance encounter on a country road is in truth an appointment, kept by God with the man to whom he has determined to give ten tribes.
30:
The prophetic sign is performed, and the witnesses read the torn garment as the visible enactment of the divine sentence, dividing gently over whose cloak it was. The act is startling — Ahijah caught the new garment that was on him, and rent it in twelve pieces. The Reformed Gill weighs the ambiguity of the pronoun: the words look as if the garment were Jeroboam's, newly got to appear before the king, though the sense may equally be that the prophet took hold of the garment upon himself. The grammatical-historical Ellicott decides for the latter — it was Ahijah's rending of his own new garment, used, like Saul's rending of Samuel's mantle (1 Samuel 15:27–28), to symbolise the rending away of the kingdom.

Ellicott places the deed within the whole tradition of prophetic sign-acts, noting how frequent such symbolism becomes in the later prophets — the marred girdle, the broken bottle, the yokes and the models of siege (Jeremiah 13:1; Jeremiah 19:1; Ezekiel 4:5). The object of these acted parables, he says, is to arrest attention and call out the inquiry, Wilt thou not tell us what these things are to us? (Ezekiel 24:19). The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose account gathers the following verses, simply carry the sign into its interpretation: the ten pieces given to Jeroboam are the ten tribes torn from Solomon's hand, one tribe reserved for David's sake and for Jerusalem's.

The witnesses' small disagreement over the garment is not idle, for it touches the force of the sign. If the cloak was Ahijah's own, as Ellicott and the parallel with Samuel suggest, then the prophet gives away pieces of what was truly his, and the gift of ten pieces of another's garment carries a fuller weight. Either way, the commentators agree on what the deed declares: that the kingdom is a single fabric, woven of twelve tribes, which the God who made it may Himself unmake; and that the tearing is not Jeroboam's ambition seizing what it can, but the deliberate, announced act of the Lord, performed before two witnesses in a lonely field, so that when the division comes it will be known to have been foretold and foreordained.
31:
The meaning of the torn garment is now spoken, and the witnesses read the verse as the plain declaration of the coming division of the kingdom. The prophet's word is direct — Take thee ten pieces; for thus saith the Lord, the God of Israel, Behold, I will rend the kingdom out of the hand of Solomon, and will give ten tribes to thee. The Reformed Gill reads the sign in exact proportion: ten of the twelve pieces are handed over as an emblem of the ten tribes Jeroboam is to receive.

The Wesleyan Clarke lays out the whole symbolism piece by piece. The garment was the symbol of the kingdom of Israel; the twelve pieces were the symbol of the twelve tribes; the ten pieces given to Jeroboam were the symbol of the ten tribes that should be given to him, to form afterward the kingdom of Israel ruling in Samaria, distinguished from the kingdom of Judah ruling in Jerusalem. The acted parable is thus a map of the nation's whole future, drawn in the torn cloth of a single cloak.

The witnesses do not divide here; the verse is too clear for dispute, and its very clarity is the point. What had been announced to Solomon as sentence is now announced to Jeroboam as gift, and both hear the same divine I will rend. The commentators feel the sobering symmetry: the kingdom is not seized by a rebel but bestowed by God, torn from one hand and placed in another by the same sovereign will. Ten tribes to Jeroboam, two reserved to David's line — the numbers that will govern the rest of the history of the two kingdoms are fixed here, in a field, by a prophet's word. And the reader is taught that the schism to come, for all the human ambition and folly that will attend its outbreak, has its origin not in the intrigues of men but in the announced purpose of the God of Israel, who gives kingdoms and takes them away, and who here, through Ahijah, hands Jeroboam his ten pieces of the rent robe.
32:
The reservation of the single tribe is stated, and the witnesses read it as the mercy clause that keeps the lamp of David burning. Set against the ten pieces given away stands the exception — but he shall have one tribe for my servant David's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake, the city which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel. The Reformed Gill accounts for the arithmetic that reckons two tribes as one, noting that Judah and Benjamin are counted together, little Benjamin being in effect absorbed into the great tribe of Judah.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, whose exposition gathers these verses, dwells on the mercy the reservation embodies. Two tribes — called one because Benjamin was as good as lost in the thousands of Judah — remain sure to the house of David, and Jeroboam must never attempt them. This is done, Henry says, that David may have a lamp, a shining name and memory (Psalms 132:17), so that his royal family may not be extinct. David is not rejected as Saul was; God would not take his loving-kindness from him. The house of David must be supported and kept in reputation through all this, for out of it the Messiah must arise; and Henry hears in the reservation the echo of the vineyard God will not wholly destroy — destroy it not, for a blessing is in it (Isaiah 65:8).

The witnesses do not divide. Gill supplies the count, Henry the meaning, and both agree that the one tribe is spared not for its own worth but for a purpose reaching far beyond it. In the midst of judgment God fences off a remnant, and the fence is drawn around Jerusalem and the line of David because the promise of a coming King runs through them. The verse thus holds together, in a single breath, the severity that gives ten tribes to a servant and the fidelity that keeps one for the Son of David yet unborn. The kingdom is torn, but the thread of redemption is not; and the commentators would have the reader see, in this smallest of mercies granted to Solomon's ruined house, the patience of a God who chastens without casting off, and who guards the flickering lamp of David against the darkness of the schism to come.
33:
The ground of the judgment is now spelled out, and the witnesses read the verse as the divine indictment that names both the sin and the sinners. The reason is given plainly — because that they have forsaken me, and have worshipped Ashtoreth the goddess of the Zidonians, Chemosh the god of the Moabites, and Milcom the god of the children of Ammon, and have not walked in my ways. The Reformed Gill reads forsaken me as forsaken my worship, following the Targum, and observes that the plural includes both Solomon and the children of Israel who followed his example — no great wonder, he adds, considering how prone the people always were to idolatry.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch let the roll of the three deities stand as the concrete substance of the charge: Ashtoreth, Chemosh, Milcom — the very gods whose high places Solomon had built — set over against the David who kept the Lord's statutes and judgments.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the sobering logic of the verse. The reason the kingdom is given to Jeroboam is not his good character or desert but the chastising of Solomon's apostasy: because he, and his family, and many of his people with him, have forsaken me and worshipped other gods. It was because they had done ill, Henry insists, not because Jeroboam was likely to do much better — just as Israel was told that it was not for their righteousness that they were made masters of Canaan, but for the wickedness of the Canaanites (Deuteronomy 9:4). Jeroboam did not deserve so good a post, but Israel deserved so bad a prince. And in this Henry hears a warning aimed at Jeroboam himself: the God who rent the kingdom from Solomon's house for forsaking him could as easily rend it from Jeroboam's, should he sin away his preferment in like manner. The witnesses agree without dispute. The verse fixes the whole tragedy upon a single cause — a heart and a nation that forsook the Lord — and refuses every flattering explanation, so that the elevation of Jeroboam is exposed as no reward of virtue but the instrument of a righteous chastening.
34:
The mercy of delay is reaffirmed, and the witnesses read the verse as the divine restraint that keeps Solomon on his throne to the end of his life. The word limits the stroke — Howbeit I will not take the whole kingdom out of his hand, but I will make him prince all the days of his life, for David my servant's sake, whom I chose, because he kept my commandments and my statutes. The Reformed Gill marks the plain sense: God will not take any part of the kingdom out of Solomon's own hand during his lifetime.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the patience and the purpose of the arrangement. Solomon is to keep possession during his life, and therefore Jeroboam must not offer to dethrone him but wait with patience until his day come to fall. And Henry marks precisely the ground on which Solomon retains the crown: it is not for his own sake, for he had forfeited his crown to the justice of God, but for David my servant's sake, because he kept my commandments. From this he draws a tender observation of providence — that children who do not tread in their parents' steps yet often fare the better in this world for their good parents' piety. Solomon reigns to his last day on the strength of his father's faithfulness, not his own.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill fixes the extent of the reprieve, Henry its reason and its lesson, and both hear in the verse a mercy that is real yet reserved. The kingdom is not torn from Solomon; he dies a king, his throne intact, his reign undisturbed in its substance for all the adversaries at its edges. Yet the mercy leaves the sentence standing, only postponed, and it rests wholly upon David. The commentators would have the reader feel both the kindness and the sobriety of it: that God does not deal with Solomon as his sin deserved, sparing him the sight of the division; and that even this sparing is a debt paid to a father's covenant faithfulness, a reminder that the favour a man enjoys may be owed less to himself than to the piety of those who went before him.
35:
The timing of the transfer is fixed to the next generation, and the witnesses read the verse as the appointment of the son to bear what the father was spared. The word is exact — but I will take the kingdom out of his son's hand, and will give it unto thee, even ten tribes. The Reformed Gill notes the extent of it: all shall be taken from the son except the tribes of Judah and Benjamin, the ten passing to Jeroboam.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose account carries these verses together, set the clause within the whole shape of the sentence: Solomon prince all his life for David's sake, then the kingdom rent from his son's hand, ten tribes given to Jeroboam, one reserved to David's line. The judgment is thus staged across two reigns — deferred through Solomon's lifetime, executed in Rehoboam's — so that the stroke falls a generation late.

The witnesses do not contend; the verse offers no crux. But its plainness carries a weight the commentators feel. The son will lose what the father kept, and lose it precisely because the father sinned — a hard providence in which the mercy shown to Solomon becomes the burden of Rehoboam. Matthew Henry's counsel from the surrounding verses hangs over the clause: Jeroboam must wait with patience, not stretching out his hand against the living king, but abiding God's time. The transfer is certain, its beneficiary named, its extent measured to ten tribes; yet its hour is set beyond Solomon's death. The reader is taught the long patience of the divine dealings — that God's sentences may be spoken in one reign and executed in the next, that the guilty may be spared the sight of the ruin their sin has earned, and that the man to whom a kingdom is promised must nonetheless learn to wait, holding the promise without seizing it, until the God who gave the word brings it to pass in his own appointed season upon the son. Gill's careful reckoning that Judah and Benjamin alone remain to David's house underlines the proportion of the whole: ten tribes torn away, two preserved, the schism that will define the rest of the two kingdoms already numbered and dated here in the prophet's word, waiting only for Solomon to sleep with his fathers before it breaks upon Rehoboam.
36:
The purpose of the one reserved tribe is unfolded, and the witnesses read the verse as the promise of an unfailing lamp for David's house. The word is warm with covenant meaning — unto his son will I give one tribe, that David my servant may have a light alway before me in Jerusalem, the city which I have chosen me to put my name there. The Reformed Gill unpacks the figure of the light: it means a kingdom, as the Targum renders it, an illustrious prince and successor shining in royal majesty to guide and comfort the people, an honour to David's family and a means of continuing the pure worship of God in the temple at Jerusalem.

The Wesleyan Clarke lifts the lamp to its farthest reach. A light alway means that David's posterity shall never fail and the regal line never become extinct; and this, he says, was in reference to the Messiah, who was David's light, and more — a light to enlighten the Gentiles (Luke 2:32). The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the same phrase as the lamp of David that God had sworn to keep burning (Psalms 132:17), the token that the house of David would not be quenched.

The witnesses converge with unusual fullness. Gill fixes the immediate sense — a continuing prince and a preserved worship; Henry roots it in the oath sworn to David; Clarke carries it to its Messianic term. And their agreement gives the verse its depth. The single tribe is not merely a political remnant but a burning lamp, kept alight in the chosen city where God has set his name, so that the darkness of the schism shall never wholly close over David's line. Behind the arrangement stands a purpose older than Solomon's sin and larger than Rehoboam's diminished kingdom: the coming of the Son of David, the true and everlasting light. The commentators would have the reader see that the mercy shown here is not sentiment but fidelity — God keeping his word to David, and through David keeping his word to the world, guarding in Jerusalem the flame from which the Light of the nations would one day be kindled.
37:
The promise turns now directly upon Jeroboam himself, and the witnesses read the verse as a grant fitted to a man's own ambition. The word is personal and generous — I will take thee, and thou shalt reign according to all that thy soul desireth, and shalt be king over Israel. The Reformed Gill marks the elevation: God will take Jeroboam from the low estate in which he stood and make him a king, raising the widow's son and Solomon's servant to a throne.

The Wesleyan Clarke fastens on the revealing phrase all that thy soul desireth. It appears from this, he argues, that Jeroboam had already affected the kingdom and was seeking an opportunity to seize the government; and God now tells him, by his prophet, exactly what he shall have and what he shall not have, in order to prevent his attempting to grasp the whole to the prejudice of the spiritual seed of David. The promise, on Clarke's reading, is both gift and restraint — it satisfies the ambition it acknowledges, while bounding it, so that Jeroboam shall have ten tribes and no more.

The witnesses do not divide, but their combined reading gives the verse its edge. That God should promise a man according to all that thy soul desireth is a mercy shadowed by a warning, for the desire in question was already reaching toward a crown. The Lord meets Jeroboam's aspiration not by rebuking it outright but by channelling it, granting the ten tribes his heart was set upon while reserving the two he must never touch. The commentators feel the delicacy of the arrangement: God condescends to the man's longing, yet fences it with a limit, lest ambition unchecked should overthrow the very line through which redemption must come. Jeroboam is given a kingdom to satisfy his soul, and told in the same breath where its borders lie; and the reader is left to wonder whether a man so plainly ambitious, so exactly indulged, will keep within the bound that grace has set — a question the following verses press, and that Jeroboam's later history will answer to his ruin.
38:
