The promise to Jeroboam is made conditional, and the witnesses read the verse as a covenant offered on the same terms once given to David. The word is weighty with possibility — if thou wilt hearken unto all that I command thee, and wilt walk in my ways, and do that is right in my sight, to keep my statutes and my commandments, as David my servant did; that I will be with thee, and build thee a sure house, as I built for David, and will give Israel unto thee. The Reformed Gill takes the condition simply: if Jeroboam will hearken to all God commands and be obedient thereunto.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose account carries these verses to their close, set the condition within the whole oracle: walk in my ways and keep my statutes as David did, and I will build thee a sure house and give thee Israel. They are careful to add that though God foretold and permitted Jeroboam's reign, this did not justify his rebellion; the promise was conditional grace, not a warrant for the lifting up of the hand against the living king.

The Wesleyan Clarke reads the offer for the tragedy it foreshadows. God would have continued his posterity on the throne of Israel, he says, had Jeroboam not by his wickedness forfeited the promises of God and thrown himself out of the protection of the Most High. The sure house was really held out to him; it was not withheld by God but lost by Jeroboam. The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the extraordinary spectacle of a Davidic-style covenant offered to the man who would tear the kingdom away — the same promise of a sure house, the same condition of obedience, the same presence of God pledged on the same terms. And they feel the pathos of it keenly, knowing what Jeroboam will do: set up his calves at Dan and Bethel, and so make Israel to sin. The offer stands as a lasting proof that his ruin was self-chosen. God's part was faithful; the sure house was forfeited, not refused, by the man who would not walk in the ways of the David whose throne he inherited.
39:
The oracle closes with a word that reaches beyond judgment to hope, and the witnesses read the verse as the promise that David's affliction shall not be endless. The word is brief but far-seeing — and I will for this afflict the seed of David, but not for ever. The Reformed Gill fixes the cause of the affliction: it is for this, for the idolatry Solomon had been guilty of and had connived at, that the seed of David must be brought low.

The Wesleyan Clarke seizes upon the qualifying clause but not for ever and carries it to its Messianic term. The seed of David, he says, shall be in affliction and distress only until the Messiah come, who shall sit upon the throne of David to order it and establish it in judgment and justice for ever. He even reaches for a Jewish witness to the same hope, citing Jarchi's comment that when the Messiah comes, the kingdom shall be restored to the house of David.

The witnesses do not divide, and their brief notes together open a wide horizon. The verse grants that judgment is real — David's line will be humbled, and humbled for Solomon's sin — but it fences the judgment with a limit. The affliction has a term; it is not for ever. And the commentators, Reformed and Wesleyan alike, hear in that limit the promise of the coming King in whom the diminished throne will be raised again to an everlasting dominion. The chapter that began with the wisest king's fall thus ends its great oracle looking past the ruin to a restoration, past Rehoboam's shrunken kingdom to the Son of David whose reign shall not be torn away. Even in pronouncing the affliction of David's seed, God keeps the door of hope open; the lamp reserved in Jerusalem will not be quenched, and the very sentence upon the house of David carries within it the pledge that its humiliation is temporary and its glory sure. Gill's insistence that the affliction is for this, for the connived idolatry, keeps the justice of the stroke in view even as Clarke and Jarchi look past it; the two readings do not clash but complete each other, the one naming the sin that earned the chastening, the other the King in whom the chastening ends.
40:
Solomon's response to the prophecy closes the Jeroboam episode, and the witnesses read the verse as a study in the folly of fighting the decree of God. The king's reaction is swift and futile — Solomon sought therefore to kill Jeroboam; and Jeroboam arose, and fled into Egypt, unto Shishak king of Egypt, and was in Egypt until the death of Solomon. The Reformed Gill calls it another instance of Solomon's folly, to seek to defeat the counsel of God when he had himself been assured that the kingdom should be rent and given to his servant; and he supposes Solomon learned of the matter either through Ahijah's making no secret of it, or through Jeroboam's inability to keep his own counsel, or through the report of the servants.

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the irony to its sharpest point. Solomon foolishly sought to kill his successor. Had he not taught others that whatever devices are in men's hearts, the counsel of the Lord shall stand (Proverbs 19:21)? And yet does he himself now think to defeat that counsel? Jeroboam, by contrast, prudently withdrew into Egypt, content to live in exile and obscurity for a while, being sure of a kingdom at last — and Henry turns it upon the reader: shall not we be content, who have a better kingdom in reserve?

The grammatical-historical commentators anchor the flight in history. Clarke notes that Shishak is the first proper name of an Egyptian king in Scripture, Pharaoh being the common title, and Ellicott identifies him securely with the Sheshenk of the monuments, founder of a new Bubastite dynasty about 980 B.C., who reigned through the last fifteen years of Solomon and would later invade Judah in Rehoboam's fifth year (1 Kings 14:25). His favourable reception of the rebel Jeroboam, Ellicott adds, marks a natural change of Egypt's attitude toward the Israelite power. The witnesses agree in the lesson. Solomon, the teacher of providence, forgets his own doctrine and takes the sword against a decree of God; Jeroboam waits in patient exile for what is promised. The wise man fights the counsel of the Lord in vain, and the very attempt to kill the successor only sends him to a throne of refuge, kept safe in Egypt until the king who sought his life should sleep with his fathers.
41:
The formal conclusion of Solomon's reign opens here, and the witnesses read the notice of his annals for what it tells of the sources behind the sacred history. The verse points beyond itself — the rest of the acts of Solomon, and all that he did, and his wisdom, are they not written in the book of the acts of Solomon? The Reformed Gill weighs the authorship, suggesting the book was written either by Solomon himself, as Kimchi thought, though no longer extant, or by some appointed chronicler, or by several prophets, out of whose records the inspired writer took what God directed him to transmit (2 Chronicles 9:29).

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the reference the same way: it points to another history, then extant but since lost because not divinely inspired, probably compiled by a court chronologer, out of which the sacred writer extracted what God saw fit to preserve for the church. The Wesleyan Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott fill in the names from the parallel record — the book of Nathan the prophet, the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, and the visions of Iddo the seer (2 Chronicles 9:29). Ellicott sees the prophets here appearing as annalists, Nathan covering the early years, Ahijah much of the later reign, and Iddo the closing acts; and he judges the narrative of Kings a compilation drawn from various sources — a temple record behind the account of the building, official archives behind the notes on territory and administration.

Keil and Delitzsch, also of the grammatical-historical school, raise here the question that the last verses leave hanging: whether Solomon at the end turned again to the Lord with all his heart cannot be ascertained from Scripture. If the book of Ecclesiastes may be traced to him, they allow, it would be evidence at least that at the close of life he discovered the vanity of all earthly possessions and declared the fear of God the only abiding good. The witnesses do not contend over the plain sense. They agree that the fuller story of Solomon lay in records now perished, kept by the prophets who served as the historians of the age, and that the Spirit preserved from them only what the church needed. And they let the reference sound its quiet, sobering note: the wisest of kings filled a book with his acts and his wisdom, and that book is lost, while the one thing the Spirit thought fit to record at length is the story of his fall.
42:
The length of the reign is registered, and the witnesses read the round figure of forty years against a tangle of competing chronologies. The notice is plain — the time that Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel was forty years. The Reformed Gill uses the number to correct the traditions on either side. Against the common Jewish tradition that Solomon was but twelve at his accession he sets the age of his son Rehoboam, forty-one when he began to reign, which forbids so young a father; and against Josephus, who makes Solomon reign eighty years and live to ninety-four, he objects that this stretches his life too far, as the heathen writer Eupolemus made him live only fifty-two.

The Wesleyan Clarke works the same crux to a moderate estimate. Josephus's fourscore years he calls absurd; following Calmet, he supposes Solomon eighteen at his accession and dead at fifty-eight — and, considering the excess in which he lived and the criminal passions he indulged among his thousand wives, judges that this life was as long as could be reasonably expected. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the curious coincidence that Solomon's reign was exactly as long as his father's (1 Kings 2:11), yet insists the accurate character of the whole narrative forbids treating forty as a mere round number for long duration; Josephus's eighty he ascribes to a textual error or to a confusion of the two reigns.

Ellicott also records here the long alternative account of Jeroboam's early history preserved in the Greek text, which makes his mother a harlot, credits him with three hundred chariots and open royal pretensions, and reworks the story of the torn garment under another prophet's name. He judges it far inferior in authority to the Hebrew — its incidents fitting less naturally together, its details plainly imitated from the story of Hadad — and notes that Josephus ignores it altogether. The witnesses do not divide on the plain sense. They read the forty years both as a sober fact of history, matching David's reign, and as a life that sin had shortened; and they let the various reckonings of Solomon's age serve one quiet moral — that the wisest of men, for all his glory, did not live long, and that his excesses hastened the decay of the very years his wisdom might have prolonged.
43:
The chapter ends at a grave, and over it the witnesses hold the book's most searching debate: whether Solomon died a saved man. The notice is spare — Solomon slept with his fathers, and was buried in the city of David his father; and Rehoboam his son reigned in his stead. The Reformed Gill glosses only that he died as they did. The Puritan Matthew Henry marks the sober comparison that his reign was as long as his father's but his life was not, for sin shortened his days; and he draws the great disappointment of the man who had everything — if the world with all its advantages could satisfy the soul, Solomon would have found it so, but he was disappointed in all, and has left us the record of it in his verdict, vanity and vexation of spirit. Henry lifts the reader at once to the greater Son: one greater than Solomon is come to reign and to possess the throne of his father David, and in this figure we may see something of Christ's excellency faintly represented.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns the burial into a long and unsparing retrospective, and it is here the great debate is joined. He honours the youth loved of God and named Jedidiah, the piety proved by the prayer for wisdom and the prayer at the temple, the universal knowledge, the poetry of the Song, the natural history (1 Kings 4:33). But he laments the oppressive taxation that laid the foundation of the schism, and cries with the prophet, O Lucifer, son of the morning, how art thou fallen! His sharpest word falls on the question of repentance. Many hold that Solomon repented and wrote Ecclesiastes after his conversion; Clarke finds not the shadow of a proof of it. The book is no penitent's language, he says, showing none of the broken-hearted confession of David's psalms or Jeremiah's lamentations; and Scripture, recording his apostasy and his death in the same breath, gives no hint that he ever turned again. He denies even that Solomon was a type of Christ, urging that types may perish as the brazen serpent did, honoured once and then broken as a mere Nehushtan (John 3:14; 2 Kings 18:4). Solomon, he concludes, died in his sins, under the sentence, if thou forsake the Lord, he will cast thee off for ever (1 Chronicles 28:9); and he presses it against the doctrine of unconditional perseverance — let him that standeth take heed lest he fall, not only foully but finally.

The witnesses genuinely divide. The Puritan Henry had elsewhere reasoned that Solomon repented and published his repentance in Ecclesiastes, his penitential sermon; the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch judged that the case cannot be settled from Scripture, though Ecclesiastes, if his, shows at least a man who discovered the vanity of the world and returned to the fear of God. Clarke alone reads the silence as condemnation. Beneath the debate, however, the witnesses land together on one warning, the refrain of the whole chapter — that the wisest and most favoured of men fell, and that the Scripture leaves his end doubtful on purpose, so that none should sin upon the presumption of repenting. Let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall (1 Corinthians 10:12): that, and not any confident verdict on Solomon's soul, is the lesson the commentators would have the reader carry from his grave.
